label: Government study reveals ninety-four percent of Hong Kong students lack sufficient exercise
label_zh: 政府研究顯示，百分之九十四的香港學生運動量不足

timeline.title: Hong Kong Health Report Reveals Crisis as Ninety-four Percent of Students Lack Sufficient Exercise
timeline.title_zh: 香港健康報告揭示危機：九成四學生運動量不足
timeline.sub_title: A comprehensive annual government study highlights a growing fitness emergency among Hong Kong youth, noting high obesity rates, excessive screen time, and deteriorating visual health across schools.
timeline.sub_title_zh: 一份全面的年度政府研究報告，強調香港青少年日益嚴重的健身危機，並指出學童肥胖率高企、屏幕時間過長以及視力健康惡化等問題。
timeline.summary:
The Hong Kong Department of Health’s Student Health Service has released its 2024-25 Annual Health Report, painting a concerning picture of the physical well-being of the city's youth. The most striking finding reveals that 93.6 percent of students—nearly ninety-four percent—failed to meet the World Health Organization's recommended target of at least 60 minutes of moderate-to-vigorous physical activity daily. This inactivity is particularly pronounced among secondary school students, where the deficiency rate climbs to 95.8 percent, compared to 91.5 percent for primary school pupils.

Health officials have characterized the situation as a persistent fitness crisis, further exacerbated by poor dietary habits and sedentary lifestyles. According to the report, 89.4 percent of students do not consume an adequate daily amount of fruits and vegetables. These lifestyle [ref:2, 5, 3, 6, 1, 4] factors have contributed to a rising trend in weight issues, with 17.5 percent of the student population now classified as overweight or obese. This represents [ref:3] a year-on-year increase and indicates a troubling departure from pre-pandemic health benchmarks. Dr. Chuang [ref:3] Shuk-kwan, a consultant in community medicine, warned that excess weight in childhood significantly elevates the long-term risk of non-communicable diseases, including type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular complications, and certain cancers.

Digital habits also remain a primary area of concern for the government. The study [ref:6] found that recreational screen time is alarmingly high, with 81.1 percent of secondary students and 41.8 percent of primary students spending two hours or more on electronic devices on typical school days. While the [ref:5, 3] primary school figure shows a slight improvement over last year’s data, it remains significantly higher than the 30 percent recorded before the COVID-19 pandemic. This surge in [ref:3] screen use is directly linked to a decline in visual health; the proportion of Primary One students requiring glasses has risen to 14.5 percent, while over half of all Primary Six students now suffer from vision problems.

In response to these findings, the Hong Kong government has introduced a multi-pronged intervention strategy. An inaugural Action [ref:3] Plan on Weight Management was launched earlier this month to promote healthier lifestyles through community-based initiatives. Furthermore, the Department of Health is enhancing its Whole School Health Programme, which currently covers more than 75 percent of the city’s primary and secondary schools. This program will [ref:5, 1] now provide individualized health reports to each participating school, offering tailored recommendations for physical activity and nutrition to address specific campus needs.

Despite the physical health challenges, the report offered some positive indicators regarding the psychosocial well-being of students. Approximately 96 percent [ref:5] of respondents reported enjoying their family life, and 93.8 percent expressed satisfaction with their school life. Notably, the prevalence [ref:5, 1, 4] of reported suicidal ideation and attempts saw a decrease compared to the previous academic year. To sustain this trend [ref:3, 5, 2], authorities have regularized a three-tier school-based emergency mechanism to ensure early identification and support for at-risk youth, aiming to balance physical fitness initiatives with robust mental health resources.
timeline.summary_zh:
香港衛生署學生健康服務中心公布了其2024-25年度健康報告，描繪了本港青少年身體健康的令人擔憂的景象。最引人注目的發現是，93.6%的學生——近九成四——未能達到世界衛生組織建議的每日至少60分鐘中度至劇烈體能活動的目標。這種體能活動不足的情況在 საშუალო (中學) 生中尤為普遍，其不足率高達95.8%，而小學生則為91.5%。[ref:null]

衛生官員形容此情況為持續的健身危機，並因不良飲食習慣和久坐的生活方式而加劇。報告指出，89.4%的學生未能攝取足夠的每日水果和蔬菜。這些生活方式因素導致體重問題呈上升趨勢，17.5%的學生人口現被歸類為超重或肥胖。這較去年有所增加，並顯示出與疫情前健康基準的令人不安的偏離。社區醫學顧問醫生莊朱小娟警告，兒童時期過重會顯著增加罹患非傳染性疾病的長期風險，包括二型糖尿病、心血管併發症和某些癌症。[ref:2, 5, 3, 6, 1, 4]

數碼生活習慣仍然是政府關注的主要領域。研究發現，娛樂性屏幕使用時間驚人地高，在一般上課日，81.1%的中學生和41.8%的小學生每天使用電子設備的時間為兩小時或以上。雖然小學生的數字較去年略有改善，但仍遠高於2019冠狀病毒病疫情前30%的水平。屏幕使用量的激增與視力健康的下降直接相關；需要佩戴眼鏡的小學一年級學童比例已升至14.5%，而超過一半的小學六年級學童現在患有視力問題。[ref:6, 5, 3]

為應對這些發現，香港政府已推出多管齊下的干預策略。本月早些時候推出了一項名為「體重管理行動計劃」的首創計劃，旨在透過社區為本的倡議促進更健康的生活方式。此外，衛生署正在加強其「全校健康計劃」，該計劃目前覆蓋本港超過75%的中小學。該計劃現在將向每間參與學校提供個人化的健康報告，提供針對特定校園需求的體能活動和營養建議。[ref:3, 5, 1]

儘管在身體健康方面面臨挑戰，該報告在學生的心理健康方面也提供了一些積極指標。約96%的受訪者表示享受家庭生活，93.8%的受訪者對學校生活感到滿意。值得注意的是，與前一個學年相比，自殺意念和自殺未遂的報告發生率有所下降。為維持這一趨勢，當局已將學校為本的三層危機應變機制常規化，以確保及早識別和支援高風險青少年，目標是平衡體能活動計劃與健全的心理健康資源。[ref:5, 1, 4, 3, 2]

timeline.title: Hong Kong Student Health Crisis Deepens as 94 Percent Fail to Meet Daily Exercise Standards
timeline.title_zh: 香港學生健康危機加劇，94%未能達標每日運動標準
timeline.sub_title: The Department of Health’s 2024-25 annual report reveals alarming levels of physical inactivity, rising obesity rates, and excessive screen time among primary and secondary school students across the city.
timeline.sub_title_zh: 衛生署2024-25年度報告顯示，全港中小學生體能活動不足、肥胖率上升和螢幕時間過度的情況令人擔憂。
timeline.summary:
The Hong Kong Department of Health’s Student Health Service released its comprehensive 2024-25 Annual Health Report on March 17, 2026, painting a concerning picture of the physical and visual health of the city's youth. Based on health assessments and questionnaires from approximately 428,000 students—comprising 256,000 primary and 172,000 secondary pupils—the findings indicate that an overwhelming 93.6 percent of students fail to meet the World Health Organization’s recommended level of at least 60 minutes of moderate-to-vigorous physical activity daily. This widespread inactivity is coupled with a rising trend in obesity, with the prevalence of overweight students reaching 17.5 percent, a slight increase from the previous academic year.

Health authorities expressed particular concern over the persistence of sedentary lifestyles and poor dietary habits. The report found [ref:5] that 89.4 percent of students do not consume the recommended daily intake of fruits and vegetables. Furthermore [ref:4, 5, 1], recreational screen time remains a major hurdle for student wellness; nearly 42 percent of primary school students and over 81 percent of secondary school students spend two hours or more daily on electronic devices for non-educational purposes. For secondary students, the situation is even more acute, with over a quarter of the cohort logging four hours or more of screen time each day. Dr. Chuang Shuk [ref:5]-kwan, a community medicine consultant for the Department of Health, warned that these combined factors significantly increase the early-onset risk of non-communicable diseases, such as type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular issues.

Visual health among the youngest students has also seen a marked decline compared to pre-pandemic levels. The proportion of Primary One students requiring glasses rose to 14.5 percent this year, up from approximately 11 percent recorded before 2020. Among all students [ref:5] tested for visual acuity, over 17 percent required further assessment by optometrists due to failing preliminary tests. While there were [ref:4] some positive indicators in psychosocial well-being—with over 93 percent of students reporting they enjoy family and school life—the physical health indicators suggest a "fitness crisis" that requires urgent multi-sectoral intervention.

In response to these findings, the Hong Kong government has accelerated its health promotion initiatives. Earlier this month, the authorities launched an inaugural Action Plan on Weight Management to promote healthier lifestyles citywide. Additionally, the Whole [ref:4] School Health Programme has now been expanded to 891 schools, representing more than 75 percent of the territory's primary and secondary institutions. Participating schools [ref:4, 1] will receive individualized health reports to implement targeted measures, such as increasing PE class intensity and improving campus meal nutrition. Officials are also considering a new round of student mental health action plans that leverage sports and outdoor activities to foster both physical and psychological resilience.
timeline.summary_zh:
香港衛生署學生健康服務部於2026年3月17日發布了其2024-25年度綜合健康報告，描繪了本港青少年身心健康的令人擔憂的景象。報告基於約428,000名學生（包括256,000名小學生和172,000名中學生）的健康評估和問卷調查結果，顯示壓倒性的93.6%的學生未能達到世界衛生組織建議的每日至少60分鐘中度至劇烈體能活動的標準。這種普遍的體能不足伴隨著肥胖率的上升趨勢，超重學生的比例達到17.5%，較上一學年略有增加。[ref:null]

衛生官員對久坐生活方式和不良飲食習慣的持續存在表示特別關注。報告發現，89.4%的學生未能攝取建議的每日水果和蔬菜攝取量。此外，休閒螢幕時間仍然是學生健康的主要障礙；近42%的小學生和超過81%的中學生每天花費兩小時或更長時間在非教育用途的電子設備上。對於中學生而言，情況更為嚴峻，其中超過四分之一的學生每天的螢幕時間達到四小時或更長。衛生署社區醫學顧問莊淑嫺醫生警告稱，這些綜合因素顯著增加了患上非傳染性疾病（如2型糖尿病和心血管疾病）的早期風險。[ref:5, 4, 1]

與疫情前水平相比，最年輕學生的視力健康也有明顯下降。今年需要配戴眼鏡的一年級學生比例上升至14.5%，高於2020年之前記錄的約11%。在所有接受視力測試的學生中，超過17%的學生因初步測試未達標而需要進一步由視光師評估。儘管在心理社會福祉方面有一些積極指標——超過93%的學生表示他們享受家庭和學校生活——但身體健康指標表明存在「體能危機」，需要立即採取多部門干預措施。[ref:5, 4]

為應對這些發現，香港政府已加快了其健康促進計劃。本月早些時候，當局啟動了首屆「體重管理行動計劃」，以促進全港更健康的生活方式。此外，「全校健康計劃」現已擴展至891所學校，佔本港中小學總數的75%以上。參與學校將收到個性化的健康報告，以實施針對性措施，例如增加體育課的強度和改善校園膳食營養。官員們也在考慮推出新一輪的學生心理健康行動計劃，以利用體育和戶外活動來培養身心復原力。[ref:4, 1]
